_status_vocabulary._note: Canonical status vocabulary for the portal (Rule RES-10 + RES-VS + RES-22). Narrative and captions may only use labels from this list. New labels must be added here in the same handoff cycle. Key canonicalized to `_status_vocabulary` in Ray's Wave 5C retro-apply (2026-04-19) to match the narrative_frontmatter.schema.json cross-reference and the META-ELI5 rule 3 pointer (previously the key was `status_labels` — see regression_note_20260419.md Ray's Wave 5C block).

Available.eli5: The chart, data file, or signal is produced and live on the portal — you can read it now.
Available.technical: The artifact has been produced and validated. No schema has been authored for it yet (see RES-22 decision table row 3), or a schema exists and validation passes.

Pending.eli5: The chart is on the to-do list but has not been made yet. You'll see a placeholder where it will go.
Pending.technical: The artifact is scheduled for production (named in a manifest or handoff) but not yet produced; rendered as a placeholder on the portal with an explicit pending caption per RES-22 rule 4 / META-UNK.

Validated.eli5: The chart has been double-checked — first by the person who made it, then by the person who uses it — and is safe to rely on.
Validated.technical: The artifact has passed producer self-check (Defense 1) and consumer reconciliation (Defense 2); validates against its schema; last modified within 60 days.

Stale.eli5: The chart or data was valid once but the source has been updated since — treat as out-of-date until someone refreshes it.
Stale.technical: Artifact exists on disk and validates against its schema, but last modified more than 60 days ago per RES-22 decision table row 2.

Draft.eli5: The chart is in progress — the person making it shared it for comments but hasn't finished yet.
Draft.technical: In-progress artifact shared internally for review but not yet validated for portal display. Producer explicitly marks work-in-progress per RES-22 rule 1.

Mature.eli5: The chart has been around and unchanged for a while — it's a reference standard you can trust.
Mature.technical: Artifact has persisted across 3+ acceptance cycles with no schema, validation, or content changes. Used as a cross-pair reference standard per META-RPD.

Unknown.eli5: Something is wrong — we can't tell whether the chart is trustworthy. Refresh the page and contact the pair owner before relying on it.
Unknown.technical: Artifact state cannot be determined (file present but schema validation raises unexpected error, or producer ownership is unclear). BLOCKING per META-UNK — must be resolved before acceptance, never shipped as a display state.

Basis point (bp).plain_english: One hundredth of a percentage point (0.01%). 100 basis points = 1%.
Basis point (bp).why_it_matters: Yields, spreads, and fees in fixed income move in fractions of a percent, so rounding to whole percentages loses meaningful information.
Basis point (bp).example: When the HY-IG spread moves from 400 bps to 800 bps it has widened by 400 bps (4 percentage points), doubling the extra yield investors demand for holding risky debt.
Basis point (bp).when_it_fails: bps notation is not a substitute for dual-notation (RES-4). Always pair at first mention with the % form so readers do not silently misread '800 bps' as 8 bps.

Credit spread.plain_english: The difference in yield between a risky bond and a safer benchmark bond; the extra return investors demand to lend to riskier borrowers.
Credit spread.why_it_matters: Widening spreads signal rising perceived default risk and typically precede equity drawdowns, so the spread serves as an early-warning indicator for stock investors.
Credit spread.example: If a BB-rated high-yield bond yields 8% and an AAA-rated investment-grade bond yields 4%, the credit spread is 400 bps (4 percentage points).
Credit spread.when_it_fails: Spreads can widen for non-credit reasons (liquidity shocks, rates repricing) — the 2022 episode widened HY-IG spreads ~200bps without a credit crisis. A pure rate-shock drawdown can bypass this signal.

HMM stress probability.plain_english: The probability (between 0 and 1) that a Hidden Markov Model assigns to the 'stressed' market state on a given day, based on credit-spread and VIX behaviour.
HMM stress probability.why_it_matters: It is the continuous, data-driven regime indicator that drives the winning strategy's position sizing — higher values mean a higher weight in cash, lower values mean higher equity exposure.
HMM stress probability.example: When the probability jumped from 0.086 on 2020-02-21 to 1.000 on 2020-02-24, the strategy moved from 98.4% equity to 0% (all cash) over two days.
HMM stress probability.when_it_fails: False alarms (brief spikes that revert within days — see 2020-01-27 COVID false-alarm in the execution log) get absorbed by the P2 Signal Strength smoothing; a pure-threshold on/off rule would whipsaw on these.

Buy-and-hold.plain_english: An investment strategy where you purchase an asset and hold it regardless of market conditions.
Buy-and-hold.why_it_matters: The simplest possible benchmark for evaluating whether an active strategy adds value after costs.
Buy-and-hold.example: Buying SPY at $100 on 2000-01-03 and holding through 2025-12-31 is the buy-and-hold benchmark used as the comparison leg for this pair's OOS Sharpe.
Buy-and-hold.when_it_fails: Works best in upward-trending markets; the -34% drawdown over 2007-2009 is the pain the credit-signal strategy is designed to avoid.

Counter-cyclical.plain_english: Moving opposite to the business or market cycle.
Counter-cyclical.why_it_matters: A counter-cyclical indicator rises when markets fall and vice versa — ideal as a warning signal rather than a momentum tracker.
Counter-cyclical.example: Credit spreads are counter-cyclical to SPY: widening spreads coincide with (or precede) equity declines. The empirical direction recorded in winner_summary.json and validated against Vera's chart line-styles per APP-DIR1 is `countercyclical`.
Counter-cyclical.when_it_fails: The relationship can flip in pure rate-shock regimes (2022) where credit and equity both fell for the same reason rather than in opposition.

Credit rating.plain_english: A grade assigned to a company's debt by rating agencies (S&P, Moody's, Fitch).
Credit rating.why_it_matters: Investment grade (AAA to BBB-) means lower default risk; high yield (BB+ and below) means higher default risk — the cut between them is the foundation of the HY-IG spread.
Credit rating.example: Apple is rated AA+ (investment grade); a leveraged-buyout issuer might be rated B+ (high yield).
Credit rating.when_it_fails: Ratings change slowly and lag market stress — bond prices often move long before rating agencies adjust.

Drawdown.plain_english: The peak-to-trough decline in the value of a portfolio or index.
Drawdown.why_it_matters: Risk-averse investors care about drawdowns at least as much as averages — a -50% drawdown requires a +100% gain to recover.
Drawdown.example: SPY's buy-and-hold drawdown over 2007-2009 was roughly -34%. The credit-signal strategy's OOS max drawdown is -10.2%, roughly one-third of buy-and-hold pain.
Drawdown.when_it_fails: Drawdown numbers depend on the sample window; a short OOS that misses the worst episode can understate tail risk.

Excess bond premium (EBP).plain_english: The component of credit spreads that cannot be explained by expected default risk — it captures investor sentiment and risk appetite.
Excess bond premium (EBP).why_it_matters: Proposed by Gilchrist & Zakrajsek (2012) as the 'cleaner' credit signal; isolates risk-aversion from fundamentals.
Excess bond premium (EBP).example: When EBP rises while default probabilities are flat, investors are pricing in risk appetite changes rather than actual default news.
Excess bond premium (EBP).when_it_fails: Estimation is model-dependent — different default-probability models yield different EBP decompositions.

Forward return.plain_english: The return over a future period from today's reference point.
Forward return.why_it_matters: Predictive regressions need a forward horizon — we ask 'does today's spread predict returns over the next N days?' rather than 'does today's spread match today's return.'
Forward return.example: A 21-day forward return is the percentage change in price over the next 21 trading days (~1 month).
Forward return.when_it_fails: Overlapping windows (a 63-day forward return today shares 62 days with tomorrow's) require HAC/HC3 standard errors — otherwise confidence is overstated.

Granger causality.plain_english: A statistical test that determines whether past values of one series help predict another series.
Granger causality.why_it_matters: Gives a directional hint at which series leads — 'X Granger-causes Y' means X has predictive information about Y beyond Y's own past.
Granger causality.example: At 3-5 day lags, HY-IG spread Granger-causes SPY returns (F ~3.0, p ~0.01); at lag 1, SPY Granger-causes HY-IG more strongly (F = 331).
Granger causality.when_it_fails: Does not prove real causation — only conditional predictability. Bidirectional results (as here) are common and need structural interpretation (Merton model).

Hidden Markov Model (HMM).plain_english: A statistical model that assumes the market is in one of several unobservable ('hidden') states — such as 'calm' or 'stressed' — each with different statistical properties.
Hidden Markov Model (HMM).why_it_matters: Lets the data find the regime boundary rather than imposing an arbitrary threshold; the continuous stress probability it outputs drives the winning strategy's position sizing.
Hidden Markov Model (HMM).example: This pair fits a 2-state Gaussian HMM on HY-IG changes + VIX; the Viterbi algorithm recovers the most-likely state sequence, and the resulting stress probability is used as the trade signal.
Hidden Markov Model (HMM).when_it_fails: HMMs are as good as the fit — out-of-sample classification can lag by a week when a new regime onsets (acceptable here because credit signals run at weeks-to-months horizons).

High-yield bonds (junk bonds).plain_english: Bonds from companies with credit ratings below investment grade (BB+ or lower).
High-yield bonds (junk bonds).why_it_matters: They offer higher yields as compensation for higher default risk — the yield on these bonds minus the yield on investment-grade bonds is the HY-IG spread.
High-yield bonds (junk bonds).example: A BB-rated bond might yield 6% when a AA-rated bond yields 4%; the gap widens when investors grow fearful.
High-yield bonds (junk bonds).when_it_fails: The high-yield universe itself is heterogeneous — BB-rated issues behave very differently from CCC-rated issues (see 'Quality spread').

HY-IG spread.plain_english: The difference between the option-adjusted spread on high-yield bonds and the option-adjusted spread on investment-grade bonds.
HY-IG spread.why_it_matters: Our primary signal variable. When it widens, investors are demanding more compensation to hold risky corporate debt — a classic rising-stress indicator.
HY-IG spread.example: BAMLH0A0HYM2 minus BAMLC0A0CM from FRED, measured in basis points. In calm periods it sits around 300 bps; in the GFC it peaked above 2,000 bps.
HY-IG spread.when_it_fails: During pure rate-shock drawdowns (2022), HY-IG widened only modestly (300 → 500 bps) while SPY fell -25% — the failure case that motivates the RES-20 triad.

Impulse response.plain_english: A measure of how one variable responds over time to a one-time shock in another variable.
Impulse response.why_it_matters: Shows whether effects are immediate, delayed, persistent, or transient — the shape tells a richer story than a single coefficient.
Impulse response.example: A 1-unit shock to HY-IG today drags cumulative SPY return -0.85% at the 63-day horizon, with an accelerating (drip-feed) shape visible on the local-projections chart.
Impulse response.when_it_fails: Model-dependent: VAR-based IRFs require correct system specification; local projections trade efficiency for robustness and are preferred here.

In-sample / Out-of-sample.plain_english: In-sample data is used to build and fit models. Out-of-sample data is held back and used only to test whether the model works on data it has never seen.
In-sample / Out-of-sample.why_it_matters: The gold standard for model validation — without OOS testing, apparent 'edge' may be curve-fitting.
In-sample / Out-of-sample.example: This pair's in-sample window is 2000-01 through 2017-12 (18 years); the OOS window is 2018-01 through 2025-12 (8 years). The 1.27 Sharpe is the OOS value.
In-sample / Out-of-sample.when_it_fails: Short OOS windows (<5 years) inflate Sharpe estimates — the 8-year OOS here is considered generous.

Investment-grade bonds.plain_english: Bonds from companies with credit ratings of BBB- or above.
Investment-grade bonds.why_it_matters: Considered safer, with lower yields — the benchmark against which high-yield bonds are compared to compute the HY-IG spread.
Investment-grade bonds.example: Johnson & Johnson (AAA) and Apple (AA+) issue investment-grade debt; their OAS is tracked by BAMLC0A0CM.
Investment-grade bonds.when_it_fails: Even IG bonds widen in crisis (BBB-rated issues widened ~200bps during the GFC) — the 'safe' label is relative.

Local projection.plain_english: A method for estimating impulse responses by running separate regressions at each forecast horizon, rather than specifying the entire system at once.
Local projection.why_it_matters: More robust to misspecification than VAR-based IRFs — critical when the underlying relationship is regime-dependent (as credit-equity is).
Local projection.example: Developed by Jorda (2005); applied here at horizons h = 5, 21, 63 days with HC3 robust standard errors for overlapping windows.
Local projection.when_it_fails: Less efficient than a correctly-specified VAR (standard errors are wider). The trade-off is accepted here because the system is known to be regime-dependent.

Markov-switching model.plain_english: A regression where the coefficients change depending on which regime is active.
Markov-switching model.why_it_matters: Captures the regime-conditional nature of credit-equity dynamics — 'different rules in different weather.'
Markov-switching model.example: In the calm regime, HY-IG explains a modest share of equity variance; in stress, the same coefficient becomes 2-3x larger. Developed by Hamilton (1989).
Markov-switching model.when_it_fails: With only 2 regimes, boundary behaviour can be rough; 3-state variants add flexibility at the cost of parsimony.

Merton model.plain_english: A structural model of credit risk that treats a company's equity as a call option on its assets.
Merton model.why_it_matters: Explains WHY credit and equity co-move — both are claims on the same underlying firm value. Motivates the Granger-bidirectional finding.
Merton model.example: When asset value falls toward the debt level, equity (the residual claim) declines faster than proportion, and credit spreads widen — both move for the same reason. Developed by Merton (1974).
Merton model.when_it_fails: Assumes continuous trading, a single debt maturity, and lognormal asset dynamics — all violated in practice. Still the canonical framework for understanding the credit-equity link.

NFCI.plain_english: National Financial Conditions Index, published weekly by the Chicago Federal Reserve.
NFCI.why_it_matters: Aggregates ~100 risk/liquidity/leverage indicators into one index; positive values indicate tighter-than-average financial conditions.
NFCI.example: Used as a control variable and cross-check for the HY-IG signal; NFCI and HY-IG spreads typically co-move during stress episodes.
NFCI.when_it_fails: Weekly release cadence means NFCI lags daily signals like HY-IG by up to 5 trading days.

Option-adjusted spread (OAS).plain_english: A credit spread that accounts for any embedded options (like call provisions) in the bond.
Option-adjusted spread (OAS).why_it_matters: Strips out non-credit factors, providing a cleaner measure of pure credit risk than raw yield spreads.
Option-adjusted spread (OAS).example: ICE BofA publishes OAS indices for HY (BAMLH0A0HYM2) and IG (BAMLC0A0CM); we subtract to get the HY-IG spread used throughout this pair.
Option-adjusted spread (OAS).when_it_fails: OAS is model-dependent (it relies on a stochastic interest-rate model to strip option values) — different vendors report slightly different OAS for the same bond.

Quality spread.plain_english: The spread between the lowest-rated high-yield bonds (CCC) and the highest-rated high-yield bonds (BB).
Quality spread.why_it_matters: Captures stress within the riskiest corner of the credit market — the 'canary in the coal mine' that often widens before the broader HY-IG spread.
Quality spread.example: During the GFC, the CCC-BB spread widened months before the overall HY-IG spread reached crisis levels; during COVID, CCC yields spiked above 20% while BB stayed contained.
Quality spread.when_it_fails: CCC universe is small and illiquid — bid-ask noise can produce spurious CCC-BB moves that do not signal genuine stress.

Quantile regression.plain_english: A statistical method that estimates the effect of a variable on different parts of the outcome distribution (not just the average).
Quantile regression.why_it_matters: Particularly useful for understanding tail risks — the worst possible outcomes — which is where credit signals earn their keep.
Quantile regression.example: For this pair, the 5th-percentile coefficient is -0.0117 (strongly negative) while the median coefficient is ~0 — HY-IG is a tail-risk signal, not a mean-return signal. Developed by Koenker & Bassett (1978).
Quantile regression.when_it_fails: Quantile coefficients are noisier than mean coefficients; we report bootstrapped confidence intervals (1,000 resamples) to guard against overconfidence.

Regime.plain_english: A distinct state of the market characterized by its own statistical properties (mean returns, volatility, correlations).
Regime.why_it_matters: Markets switch between regimes over time — think of calm weather versus storms. Rules that work in one regime may fail in another.
Regime.example: In this pair's 2-state HMM, 'calm' and 'stressed' regimes have different credit-equity correlation structures; the strategy acts only when the stress probability exceeds 50%.
Regime.when_it_fails: Regime boundaries are model-dependent — a 2-state HMM may miss a third regime (e.g., 'crisis' vs 'normal-stress'). 3-state variants were tested and rejected on BIC parsimony grounds.

Sharpe ratio.plain_english: A measure of risk-adjusted return: (return minus risk-free rate) divided by volatility.
Sharpe ratio.why_it_matters: Higher is better — Sharpe 1.0 is generally considered good for a long-only equity strategy; above 1.2 is strong.
Sharpe ratio.example: This pair's OOS Sharpe is 1.27, versus 0.77 for buy-and-hold SPY over the same 8-year window (2018-2025). Ratio read from winner_summary.json.oos_sharpe.
Sharpe ratio.when_it_fails: Sharpe assumes normal returns — skewed or fat-tailed distributions (as credit data has) make Sharpe overstate the true risk-adjusted edge. Sortino or max-drawdown ratios complement it.

Transfer entropy.plain_english: An information-theoretic measure of directed information flow between time series.
Transfer entropy.why_it_matters: Unlike Granger causality, it captures nonlinear relationships — cases where the connection between variables strengthens or weakens depending on conditions.
Transfer entropy.example: For this pair, TE from credit to equity is 0.042 nats (p=0.004); TE from equity to credit is 0.0055 nats — a ~7.6x asymmetry that linear Granger tests miss.
Transfer entropy.when_it_fails: TE is sensitive to bin count and lag choice; results here use 6 equal-frequency bins and lag 1 day, with 500 permutation significance tests.

VIX.plain_english: The CBOE Volatility Index, often called the 'fear gauge.'
VIX.why_it_matters: Measures the market's expectation of 30-day volatility in the S&P 500, derived from option prices. Higher VIX = more expected turbulence.
VIX.example: VIX jumped from ~15 to above 80 during the 2020 COVID shock; the HMM used VIX + HY-IG as its joint observation vector.
VIX.when_it_fails: VIX is a forward-looking estimate — it can under-respond to slow-building stress (e.g., spread widening in 2007 pre-GFC, when VIX was still suppressed).

VIX term structure.plain_english: The difference between longer-dated (VIX3M, 3-month) and shorter-dated (VIX, 1-month) implied volatility.
VIX term structure.why_it_matters: When VIX3M > VIX (contango), markets are calm. When VIX > VIX3M (backwardation), near-term fear exceeds medium-term expectations.
VIX term structure.example: The VIX-VIX3M spread is a separate pair in the portal (Pair #11) — here it is referenced as a complementary regime discriminator.
VIX term structure.when_it_fails: The signal is noisy at calm-to-stress transitions; best used in combination with slower indicators like credit spreads.

Walk-forward validation.plain_english: A backtesting method that simulates real-time trading by training on past data, testing on the next window, then rolling the window forward.
Walk-forward validation.why_it_matters: Prevents lookahead bias — the most common source of false strategy performance.
Walk-forward validation.example: This pair's tournament uses walk-forward with 1-year test windows; Random Forest + SHAP uses the same protocol to rank signal-transformation importance.
Walk-forward validation.when_it_fails: Walk-forward assumes stationarity within each training window — regime shifts within a window can degrade performance.

Z-score.plain_english: A measure of how many standard deviations a value is from its recent average.
Z-score.why_it_matters: Converts raw levels into 'how unusual is this' — a 2-sigma HY-IG spread in 2010 and a 2-sigma spread in 2020 are both rare, even though the raw bps differ.
Z-score.example: This pair uses 252-day and 504-day rolling z-scores of the HY-IG spread as candidate signals; z-scored series outperform raw levels in the correlation table.
Z-score.when_it_fails: Short lookback windows (<252 days) are too sensitive to recent regime; long lookbacks (>1260 days) lose adaptivity. We use 252 and 504 as complementary middle grounds.

Tournament.plain_english: A systematic horse-race of signal-threshold-strategy combinations, ranked by out-of-sample Sharpe.
Tournament.why_it_matters: Removes cherry-picking — every combination faces the same OOS test, so winners are not just the ones the analyst liked on first sight.
Tournament.example: This pair tested ~1,000+ combinations; the top 5 were stress-tested with walk-forward, bootstrap significance, and transaction-cost sensitivity. See results/hy_ig_v2_spy/tournament_results_20260410.csv.
Tournament.when_it_fails: Multiple-comparison bias — with 1,000 combinations tested, the best OOS Sharpe is upward-biased. Walk-forward validation and Deflated Sharpe Ratio mitigate but do not eliminate this.

Stress regime.plain_english: A market state identified by the HMM as statistically distinct from calm — characterized by higher volatility, wider credit spreads, and different cross-asset correlations.
Stress regime.why_it_matters: The strategy acts only when the HMM puts high probability on this regime; calm-regime days are held at full equity exposure with no trading.
Stress regime.example: The stress regime dominated late-2001 through 2002 (Dot-Com), mid-2007 through mid-2009 (GFC), early 2020 (COVID), and parts of 2022.
Stress regime.when_it_fails: The HMM can briefly misclassify transition days (see 2020-01-27 COVID false-alarm, resolved within 2 days by the P2 Signal Strength smoothing).

Signal probability.plain_english: The continuous 0-to-1 output of the HMM telling us how confident the model is that the stress regime is active today.
Signal probability.why_it_matters: Used directly to size positions — at 50% probability the strategy holds 50% equity, at 100% probability it holds 0% (all cash). This avoids the whipsaw of hard on/off rules.
Signal probability.example: On 2020-02-21, the HMM stress probability was 0.086 (calm); on 2020-02-24 it jumped to 1.000 (full stress), driving the position from 91.4% to 0% over two trading days.
Signal probability.when_it_fails: Daily noise around the 50% threshold can cause small position adjustments that add transaction cost without economic value — mitigated but not eliminated by the scaling rule.
